title: More Server Updates

content:
Oh boy, do I have updates for y'all.

A few months ago, just a scant week and a half before winter break (and a few days before finals), my SSD died. This continues a worrying trend in which I purchase hardware only for it to die fairly quickly. In this case, my year-old SSD died, well within the warranty. The RMA process was a pain, and they wanted the following information:

  1. Company Name (If Applicable):
  2. Name (First, Last):
  3. Full Address (PO box is NOT accepted):
  4. Email:
  5. Phone Number:
  6. Product Number:
  7. Serial Number (Located on the label on the bottom of the SSD.):
  8. Detailed Reason for Repair:
  9. Troubleshooting Performed:
  10. Computer Specifications (Please provide any system information if available):
  11. Bill of Sale (Invoice or Receipt must be in an JPEG image file format or PDF. Please do not paste but attach it to your reply email):
  12. Photo of the Label on the SSD for verification (not the packaging. Please do not paste but attach it to your reply email)
  13. Please write YES in this field to denote that you have accepted our warranty terms and conditions (Listed Below):

Although that was slightly more information that I was comfortable sharing, I was willing to go through with it to get a new SSD. However, some trawling of the web, I found that the SSD model I had, a Samsung 870 EVO, was prone to failure. My trust in Samsung fell just enough to make me not go through with the RMA process and instead overspend on an "enterprise" SSD—the Kingston DC500M.

I took the read failures as an excuse to switch away from Debian to Rocky Linux 9 (because SELinux or something). Thankfully, the borg backup scripts I had set up with rsync.net recovered almost everything (except, funnily enough, my backup scripts themselves).

In not-hardware news, I have moved away from running everything native and bare-metal to running it all in Podman containers. It's a lot easier (discounting the SELinux headaches).

I've also moved most of the persistent storage for said containers to MinIO so that it is all in one place and easier to manage large pools of data (I'm looking at you, Mastodon). Some weird bugs in MinIO that caused MinIO to be very unreliable aside (fixed with Podman auto-updates, bless), I've liked it a bit more than using simple mounts. Now, I just have to figure out how to get Jellyfin to work with it...

There is now also fancy monitoring software in place through Zabbix. Why not Grafana you ask? I don't know. I'll probably be trying it out in the coming weeks. This is how I found out that yes, there is a kernel memory leak (probably).

In other tech news, I am also working on adding OpenID integration into the TheLounge IRC client. You can see progress on this Github PR.

Finally, I have many ideas for blog posts that I just haven't gotten around to because of the computer inconveniences I've had over the past month or so. I will write another one today, probably. Who knows.

Oh yeah, I'm still on Mastodon.
